texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The gas pressure drop in the bore of hollow fiber membranes has attracted much attention. Numerical methods are generally employed to deal with such complex problems. However, numerical methods cannot provide a clear picture on how the pressure drop is affected by the physical properties of fibers (e.g., fiber inner diameter, fiber length, and intrinsic permeance) and the operating conditions (e.g., downstream pressure). This article attempts to elucidate the interaction mechanism based on an analytical approach. The pressure drop in hollow fibers is formulated by an elliptic integral function, and the effectiveness factor of the transmembrane pressure is proposed to characterize the effect. Adimensionless parameter is obtained, which contains all the dominant factors responsible for generating the pressure drop in hollow fibers. It is shown that the dimensionless parameter serves as the indicator of the significance of the effect of the pressure buildup in hollow fibers. To some degree, this parameter is also a convenient tool for rationalizing hollow fiber modules design. © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
texts.text: The gas pressure drop in the bore of hollow fiber membranes has attracted much attention. Numerical methods are generally employed to deal with such complex problems. However, numerical methods cannot provide a clear picture on how the pressure drop is affected by the physical properties of fibers (e.g., fiber inner diameter, fiber length, and intrinsic permeance) and the operating conditions (e.g., downstream pressure). This article attempts to elucidate the interaction mechanism based on an analytical approach. The pressure drop in hollow fibers is formulated by an elliptic integral function, and the effectiveness factor of the transmembrane pressure is proposed to characterize the effect. Adimensionless parameter is obtained, which contains all the dominant factors responsible for generating the pressure drop in hollow fibers. It is shown that the dimensionless parameter serves as the indicator of the significance of the effect of the pressure buildup in hollow fibers. To some degree, this parameter is also a convenient tool for rationalizing hollow fiber modules design. © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membranes have played an important role in gas separations [1-3], where most of the membranes are in the hollow fiber configuration. The commercial success based on hollow fiber permeators was first achieved by Dupont [4], which was followed by Monsanto [5-7], Dow [8-10], Union Carbide [10], and Ube Industries [10-12], etc. It is generally admitted that these commercial successes resulted from the distinct features of hollow fibers, which are self-support, high-pressure resistance and high compactness.
texts.text: Membranes have played an important role in gas separations [1-3], where most of the membranes are in the hollow fiber configuration. The commercial success based on hollow fiber permeators was first achieved by Dupont [4], which was followed by Monsanto [5-7], Dow [8-10], Union Carbide [10], and Ube Industries [10-12], etc. It is generally admitted that these commercial successes resulted from the distinct features of hollow fibers, which are self-support, high-pressure resistance and high compactness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is well known that the compactness of hollow fiber separators depends largely on the size of individual hollow fibers. texts.orig: equation, hollow fibers with small inner diameters can result in big pressure drops in the bore of the fibers. As a result, the driving force for gas permeation through the fibers can be significantly reduced, depending on how small the inner diameter of the fibers is. Obviously, the tradeoff between the compactnessandtheunfavorablepressuredropshouldbereasonably compromised so as to maximize the advantages of hollow fiber membranes in applications. As a first step toward this objective, the pressure drop in hollow fiber membranes should first be investigated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Studies involving the gas pressure drop in hollow fibers have been well documented [13-20]. The central effort of these studies was devoted to formulating numerical solutions to a set of equations (i.e., the permeation equations, the mass balance equations and the Hagen-Poiseuille equation), and to elucidating the interdependence of some important design parameters (e.g., the recovery of product gas, the purity of product gas). The effect of the pressure buildup on gas permeation through the membranes, though considered in computations, was usually ignored in discussion. Most importantly, due to the 'implicit' nature of the numerical methods, the

An analytical treatment of this issue, intended for explicitly disclosing the interaction mechanism is thus necessary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some analytical effort was paid to modeling liquid permeation through permeable hollow fibers. Middleman [21] studied the liquid flow in hollow fiber filtration membranes using the perturbation approach. The equation governing the pressure drop in porous hollow fibers was obtained in the following form:
texts.text: Some analytical effort was paid to modeling liquid permeation through permeable hollow fibers. Middleman [21] studied the liquid flow in hollow fiber filtration membranes using the perturbation approach. The equation governing the pressure drop in porous hollow fibers was obtained in the following form:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: p g( z ) = C 1exp( αz ) + C 2exp( -αz ) , α = 4 R √ kµ R

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where p g( z ) stands for the gauge pressure of the fluid in hollow fibers, z the location variable, k the permeability of the porous membrane, µ the viscosity of the fluid, R the inner radius of the fibers, and C 1 and C 2 are two constants, which can be determined by two boundary conditions, i.e., the pressure, and the flow rate at the fiber inlet. Munson-McGee [22] approximated Middleman's solution using a parabolic fitting: p g( z ) = a + bz + cz 2 , since he found that the curve of p g( z ) versus z could be well represented by a parabolic function. The three constants a , b , and c in the equation were determined by the two boundary conditions at the fiber inlet, and the mass conservation equation over the length of the fibers. The fault of this treatment is that it contains three constants so that the two boundary conditions at the inlet are not enough to determine the function. It is thus impossible to predict the filtration process based on the inlet conditions and the permeation properties of the hollow fibers. Due to the compressibility of gases, the equation governing the pressure drops of liquids in permeable fibers must not apply for gases.
texts.text: where p g( z ) stands for the gauge pressure of the fluid in hollow fibers, z the location variable, k the permeability of the porous membrane, µ the viscosity of the fluid, R the inner radius of the fibers, and C 1 and C 2 are two constants, which can be determined by two boundary conditions, i.e., the pressure, and the flow rate at the fiber inlet. Munson-McGee [22] approximated Middleman's solution using a parabolic fitting: p g( z ) = a + bz + cz 2 , since he found that the curve of p g( z ) versus z could be well represented by a parabolic function. The three constants a , b , and c in the equation were determined by the two boundary conditions at the fiber inlet, and the mass conservation equation over the length of the fibers. The fault of this treatment is that it contains three constants so that the two boundary conditions at the inlet are not enough to determine the function. It is thus impossible to predict the filtration process based on the inlet conditions and the permeation properties of the hollow fibers. Due to the compressibility of gases, the equation governing the pressure drops of liquids in permeable fibers must not apply for gases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, the gas pressure drop in hollow fibers is formulated by an elliptic integral function, and the feature of the gas pressure drop in hollow fibers was accordingly investigated. Fundamentally speaking, the presence of the pressure drop in hollow fibers deceases the driving force for gas per- meation through the hollow fibers. The effectiveness factor of the transmembrane pressure/the driving force is thus proposed to characterize the effect. A dimensionless parameter was obtained through mathematical treatment. It contains all the dominant factors leading to the pressure drop in hollow fibers, and analysis shows that the dimensionless parameter is an indicator of the significance of the effect of the pressure drop, and based on the parameter, the reasonableness of hollow fiber module design can be easily tested. The significance of the rational design of hollow fiber modules was also briefly elucidated with the effect of the pressure drop on the ideal separation factor of hollow fiber modules using some industrially important gas pairs.
texts.text: In this study, the gas pressure drop in hollow fibers is formulated by an elliptic integral function, and the feature of the gas pressure drop in hollow fibers was accordingly investigated. Fundamentally speaking, the presence of the pressure drop in hollow fibers deceases the driving force for gas per- meation through the hollow fibers. The effectiveness factor of the transmembrane pressure/the driving force is thus proposed to characterize the effect. A dimensionless parameter was obtained through mathematical treatment. It contains all the dominant factors leading to the pressure drop in hollow fibers, and analysis shows that the dimensionless parameter is an indicator of the significance of the effect of the pressure drop, and based on the parameter, the reasonableness of hollow fiber module design can be easily tested. The significance of the rational design of hollow fiber modules was also briefly elucidated with the effect of the pressure drop on the ideal separation factor of hollow fiber modules using some industrially important gas pairs.

texts.orig: Fig. 1 is a schematic diagram of a hollow fiber. Both of the fiber ends are potted in tube sheets, with one end open to provide outlet for the permeate, which flows in the bore of the fiber. Due to the small inner diameter of the fiber, a pressure drop of the permeate can be rendered. According to the literature [15-19,23], the Hagen-Poiseuille equation as shown in Eq. (1) is eligible for governing gas flow in the bores of cylindrical hollow fibers with some reasonable assumptions (e.g., idea gas, laminar flow, low radial flow relative to the axial flow):

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It should be noted that two permeation patterns are involved in gas permeation through hollow fibers, i.e. the inward permeation (the feed gas permeates from fiber's outside to the bore side), and the outward permeation (the feed gas permeates from the bore side to the outside). Since the feed gas in the outward permeation pattern is pressurized, the volumetric flow rate of the feed in the bore of the fibers is considerably reduced. For this reason, the outward permeation pattern usually leads to insignificant gas pressure drops in the fibers, the outward permeation pattern is thus not considered in this study.
texts.text: It should be noted that two permeation patterns are involved in gas permeation through hollow fibers, i.e. the inward permeation (the feed gas permeates from fiber's outside to the bore side), and the outward permeation (the feed gas permeates from the bore side to the outside). Since the feed gas in the outward permeation pattern is pressurized, the volumetric flow rate of the feed in the bore of the fibers is considerably reduced. For this reason, the outward permeation pattern usually leads to insignificant gas pressure drops in the fibers, the outward permeation pattern is thus not considered in this study.

texts.orig: 3.1. Characteristics of the gas pressure drop in hollow fibers

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on Eqs. (10) and (9), the pressure of the permeate in hollow fibers can be estimated. The profiles of the gas pressure drops in three hollow fibers with different lengths (20, 30 and 40 cm) are presented in Fig. 2. The values of the parameters involved are given as follows: upstream pressure, 200 kPa; downstream pressure, 100 kPa; permeance, 3.0 × 10 -9 mol/(m 2 s Pa); temp., 293.2 K; viscosity, 1.766 × 10 -5 Pa s; D o/ D i =2.5. These parameters take the same values until otherwise specified. Clearly, the permeate pressure

The values of the parameters involved are given as follows: upstream pressure, 200 kPa; downstream pressure, 100 kPa; permeance, 3.0 × 10 -9 mol/(m 2 s Pa); temp., 293.2 K; viscosity, 1.766 × 10 -5 Pa s; D o/ D i =2.5. These parameters take the same values until otherwise specified. Clearly, the permeate pressure

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2. The profiles of the pressure drop in fibers with different lengths. The feed pressure is 200 kPa and the downstream pressure is 100 kPa.
texts.text: Fig. 2. The profiles of the pressure drop in fibers with different lengths. The feed pressure is 200 kPa and the downstream pressure is 100 kPa.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: drops slightly over the 25% fiber length starting from the sealed end, and the sharpest pressure drops occur at the open outlets of the fibers. This results form the fact that the flow rate of the permeate increases as it flows away from the sealed end, and reaches its maximum at the fiber outlet. It seems that the pressure drop profiles can also be represented by a parabolic curve as treated by Munson-McGee [22] in dealing with liquid pressure drops in hollow fibers. To test this guess, the pressure is plotted against the squared distance from the sealed end as shown in Fig. 3(a). It is found that the parabolic curves (the solid lines) can be a good approximation of the pressure drop profiles (the dashed lines), and bigger deviations occur to longer fiber lengths (e.g., 40 cm). This supports our previous analysis: gases differ from liquids in their pressure drop behaviors due to their compressibility. When fibers are short, the pressure drops across the fibers are very small, and the gas in the fibers is very slightly compressed. The gas, in this situation, behaves like an incompressible liquid. This justifies that when the fiber length is relatively short, the gas pressure drop can be well represented by a parabolic approximation. A virtually perfect representation as shown in Fig. 3(b) of the pressure drops is obtained by a fourthorder polynomial fitting in all the three cases. The equations achieved by both types of fitting are summarized in Table 2, indicating that the gas pressure drop in hollow fibers is a
texts.text: drops slightly over the 25% fiber length starting from the sealed end, and the sharpest pressure drops occur at the open outlets of the fibers. This results form the fact that the flow rate of the permeate increases as it flows away from the sealed end, and reaches its maximum at the fiber outlet. It seems that the pressure drop profiles can also be represented by a parabolic curve as treated by Munson-McGee [22] in dealing with liquid pressure drops in hollow fibers. To test this guess, the pressure is plotted against the squared distance from the sealed end as shown in Fig. 3(a). It is found that the parabolic curves (the solid lines) can be a good approximation of the pressure drop profiles (the dashed lines), and bigger deviations occur to longer fiber lengths (e.g., 40 cm). This supports our previous analysis: gases differ from liquids in their pressure drop behaviors due to their compressibility. When fibers are short, the pressure drops across the fibers are very small, and the gas in the fibers is very slightly compressed. The gas, in this situation, behaves like an incompressible liquid. This justifies that when the fiber length is relatively short, the gas pressure drop can be well represented by a parabolic approximation. A virtually perfect representation as shown in Fig. 3(b) of the pressure drops is obtained by a fourthorder polynomial fitting in all the three cases. In case of even longer fiber lengths, the correcting function may contain even higher orders of the location variable l , since the approximation treatment, either in this work or conducted by Munson-McGee, is based on the approach of series expansion at a selected point.

texts.orig: 3.2. Dependence of the pressure drop (p * -p0) upon fiber geometry and gas permeance
texts.text: 3.2. Dependence of the pressure drop (p * -p0) upon fiber geometry and gas permeance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Eq. (10), let f ( p ) = p √ 2 p f ( p ∗ 2 -p 2 ) -3( p ∗ 3 -p 3 ) . The dimension of the integral of f ( p ) is (Pa) 1/2 , suggesting that the pressure drop ( p * -p 0) in hollow fibers may be proportional to the square of fiber length ( L 2 ) and the intrinsic gas permeance ( J ) of the membrane, and inversely proportional to the inner diameter to the power of 4. Eq. (10) shows that the relationship between the pressure drop and either of the factors mentioned above is mathematically equivalent. So only the fiber length L is selected to help elucidate the relationship, which is illustrated in Fig. 4. It can be found that the expected linear relations exist only when the pressure drop is less than 10 kPa, which is about 10% of the transmembrane pressure (100 kPa). When the pressure drop is more than 10% of the

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4. The log-log plot between the pressure buildup and squared fiber length L 2 .
texts.text: Fig. 4. The log-log plot between the pressure buildup and squared fiber length L 2 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: transmembrane pressure, the pressure drop becomes less sensitive to the increase in L 2 . Practically speaking, a big pressure buildup in hollow fibers should be avoided since it represents a sacrifice of the driving force for gas permeation. Based on Fig. 4, it can be estimated that the length of the fiber should be less than 12 cm (corresponding to the permeance of the fiber is 3.0 × 10 -9 mol/(m 2 s Pa) at 273.2 K and inner diameter is 40 𝛍 m). Otherwise, the pressure buildup in fibers will tend to significantly inhabit gas permeation. This suggests that attention should be given to the fiber length when evaluating the fibers' gas permeances. As pointed out previously, the influences of other parameters ( J and D i -4 ) are equivalent to that of the fiber length L , so fiber length should be handled with cautions particularly when the inner diameter of the fiber is small, and/or the intrinsic permeance of the fiber for the permeating gas is high.

texts.orig: 3.3. Dead-end pressure p * and the parameter λ
texts.text: 3.3. Dead-end pressure p * and the parameter λ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to Eq. (10), if the upstream and downstream pressures are given, the pressure buildup is dependent only uponthe parameter λ . The relation is depicted in Fig. 5, where both the upstream and downstream pressures are specified. For the sake of straightforwardness, the pressure buildup is expressed in the form of percentile relative to the transmembrane pressure. It can be found that the percentile increases with an increase in the parameter λ . It is reasonable, since, according to the definition of λ , an increase in λ means an increase in the length of fibers, and/or an increase in the permeance of the membrane, and/or a decrease in the inner diameter of fibers, all these bring about the increased pressure drops. Fig. 5 also shows that when λ is larger than 5000, the pressure buildup in hollow fibers increases up to the transmembrane pressure. This implies that a further increase in fiber length can no longer add to the permeate flow rate of the membranesince no permeation takes place over the increased fiber length. As a matter of fact, we are more interested in how to avoid the unfavorable pressure buildup. Fig. 5 reveals

texts.label: text
texts.orig: that the percentile is always less than 5% if λ is less than about 132. It also indicates that the feed pressure can affect this percentile, however this effect is negligible when λ is less than 132. It appears that the combined parameter λ can be employed as an index measuring the significance of the presence of the pressure drop in hollow fibers. It should be noted that the parameter λ contains only the geometric and permeating properties of hollow fibers, and it is well known that the operating conditions, particularly the downstream pressure plays an important role in determining the pressure drops. λ ≤ 132, as a criterion of the non-severity of the pressure drop in hollow fibers, is true only when the downstream pressure p 0 is 100 kPa as it is assumed in Fig. 5.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4. Effectiveness factor of the transmembrane pressure and the parameter ∆
texts.text: 3.4. Effectiveness factor of the transmembrane pressure and the parameter ∆

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dead-end pressure p * offers no direct information on how much the mass transport through the hollow fibers is affected by the pressure drop. The effectiveness factor of the transmembrane pressure defined in Eq. (13), is thus proposed for evaluating the effect. According to Eq. (15), the effectiveness factor ( φ ) of the transmembrane pressure is determined by two variables: the dimensionless parameter ∆ ( λ √ p 0 ) , and the dimensionless feed pressure P f ( p f p 0 ) . The dependence of the effectiveness factor φ on the parameter ∆ is plotted in Fig. 6. Four downstream pressures p 0 (0.1, 1, 5, and 10 MPa) are considered while the transmembrane pressure (1 MPa) is kept as constant, and the parametric variable P f in Fig. 6 thus varies from 1.1, 2, 3 to 11. At a given ∆ , a bigger P f (a lower downstream pressure) corresponds to a higher effectiveness factor. This seems to be a strange conclusion at first glance, since, according to the common knowledge, operating at a lower downstream pressure should lead to a bigger pressure drop, and thus a lower effectiveness factor. If considering the definition of the parameter ∆ = λ √ p 0 , one can realize that for a given ∆ , a lower downstream pressure corresponds to a

(15), the effectiveness factor ( φ ) of the transmembrane pressure is determined by two variables: the dimensionless parameter ∆ ( λ √ p 0 ) , and the dimensionless feed pressure P f ( p f p 0 ) . The dependence of the effectiveness factor φ on the parameter ∆ is plotted in Fig. 6. Four downstream pressures p 0 (0.1, 1, 5, and 10 MPa) are considered while the transmembrane pressure (1 MPa) is kept as constant, and the parametric variable P f in Fig. 6 thus varies from 1.1, 2, 3 to 11. At a given ∆ , a bigger P f (a lower downstream pressure) corresponds to a higher effectiveness factor. This seems to be a strange conclusion at first glance, since, according to the common knowledge, operating at a lower downstream pressure should lead to a bigger pressure drop, and thus a lower effectiveness factor. If considering the definition of the parameter ∆ = λ √ p 0 , one can realize that for a given ∆ , a lower downstream pressure corresponds to a

texts.orig: smaller λ . According to Fig. 5, a smaller λ means a smaller pressure drop, and thus a higher effectiveness factor.
texts.text: smaller λ . According to Fig. 5, a smaller λ means a smaller pressure drop, and thus a higher effectiveness factor.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: On the other hand, as expected, the effectiveness factor of the transmembrane pressure decreases as the parameter ∆ increases. It can also be seen in Fig. 6 that the effectiveness factor is essentially dependent upon the parameter ∆ alone when the effectiveness factor is very high (say higher than 90%). Since all the factors except the upstream pressure, which are responsible for the pressure drop in hollow fibers have been embodied in the parameter ∆ , the parameter ∆ is thus an indicator of the effect of the pressure drop. If a criterion: effectiveness factor φ ≥ 95%, is set as the guideline for operating hollow fiber modules in practical applications, according to Fig. 6, all the hollow fiber modules should agree with this condition: ∆ ≤ 0.4.

If a criterion: effectiveness factor φ ≥ 95%, is set as the guideline for operating hollow fiber modules in practical applications, according to Fig. 6, all the hollow fiber modules should agree with this condition: ∆ ≤ 0.4.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.5. Importance of rational design of hollow fiber modules
texts.text: 3.5. Importance of rational design of hollow fiber modules

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to the parameter ∆ , by enhancing the downstream pressure p 0, all hollow fiber modules, even those irrationally designed, can be made to operate rationally in terms of with the condition ( ∆ ≤ 0.4) satisfied. Generally speaking, operating at higher downstream pressures, and thus at higher upstream pressures can reduce membrane area, and thus save capital investment. On the other hand, it should be noted that operating at higher pressures increases the operating cost (e.g., compression cost). It is unfeasible for most gas separations where the operating cost largely determines the economic feasibility of the technology. Therefore, the proper selection of the downstream and upstream pressures should result from considering all the factors, and an optimized membrane module design is always necessary.
texts.text: According to the parameter ∆ , by enhancing the downstream pressure p 0, all hollow fiber modules, even those irrationally designed, can be made to operate rationally in terms of with the condition ( ∆ ≤ 0.4) satisfied. Generally speaking, operating at higher downstream pressures, and thus at higher upstream pressures can reduce membrane area, and thus save capital investment. On the other hand, it should be noted that operating at higher pressures increases the operating cost (e.g., compression cost). It is unfeasible for most gas separations where the operating cost largely determines the economic feasibility of the technology. Therefore, the proper selection of the downstream and upstream pressures should result from considering all the factors, and an optimized membrane module design is always necessary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To demonstrate the importance of the rational design of hollow fiber modules, the effect of the pressure drop on the permselectivity of a polysulfone hollow fiber module is presented here. The permeances of asymmetric polysulfone membranes for some industrially important gases are listed

texts.text: d CH4 is selected as the reference gas, for which the membrane module is rationally designed with the parameter ∆ equal to 0.4, and the effectiveness factor equal to 0.95.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: in Table 3. Clearly, methane and nitrogen gases have the lowest permeances compared with other gases (oxygen, carbon dioxide, helium and hydrogen). Methane and nitrogen are thus known as the so-called 'slow gas', and the others the 'fast gas'. If the slow gas methane is selected as the reference gas, and assume a hollow fiber module is rationally designed for methane permeation, therefore, the parameter ∆ is set to be 0.4 for methane, and the effectiveness factor is thus 0.95 as shown in Table 3. Due to the higher permeance of the fast gas (one minor factor is the viscosity of the gas), the effectiveness factor decreases as low as 0.44 for helium gas, and 0.54 for hydrogen gas, suggesting that almost half of the compression cost would be wasted if the module were used for separating the fast gases from the fast gas/methane mixtures. As it is well known that the pressure drop can also spoil the separating performance of membrane modules. The effect of the pressure drop on the ideal separation factor of various gas pairs is illustrated in Fig. 7. As expected, the fast gas suffers more than the slow gas (say nitrogen). For helium and hydrogen gases, the membrane becomes virtually half less selective. All these show that the rational design of hollow fiber modules for a specific separation is critically important.
texts.text: in Table 3. Clearly, methane and nitrogen gases have the lowest permeances compared with other gases (oxygen, carbon dioxide, helium and hydrogen). Methane and nitrogen are thus known as the so-called 'slow gas', and the others the 'fast gas'. If the slow gas methane is selected as the reference gas, and assume a hollow fiber module is rationally designed for methane permeation, therefore, the parameter ∆ is set to be 0.4 for methane, and the effectiveness factor is thus 0.95 as shown in Table 3. Due to the higher permeance of the fast gas (one minor factor is the viscosity of the gas), the effectiveness factor decreases as low as 0.44 for helium gas, and 0.54 for hydrogen gas, suggesting that almost half of the compression cost would be wasted if the module were used for separating the fast gases from the fast gas/methane mixtures. As it is well known that the pressure drop can also spoil the separating performance of membrane modules. The effect of the pressure drop on the ideal separation factor of various gas pairs is illustrated in Fig. 7. As expected, the fast gas suffers more than the slow gas (say nitrogen). For helium and hydrogen gases, the membrane becomes virtually half less selective. All these show that the rational design of hollow fiber modules for a specific separation is critically important.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The pressure drop in hollow fiber membranes is analytically formulated. The profile of the pressure drop across the length of a fiber can be well represented by a forth-order polynominal fitting. When the pressure buildup ( p * ) is less than 10% of the transmembrane pressure, it is proportional to the squared length of the fiber, and the intrinsic permeance of the membrane, but inversely proportional to the inner diameter to the forth power. The effectiveness factor of the transmembrane pressure is proposed to characterize the effect of the pressure drop. It was found that the effectiveness factor is dominantly determined by a dimensionless parameter ∆ , and it serves as the index measuring the significance of the effect of the pressure drop in hollow fibers. To avoid the unfavorable effect of the pressure drop, the parameter ∆ should be less than 0.4. This criterion is a very important reference for the rational design of hollow fiber permeators.
texts.text: The pressure drop in hollow fiber membranes is analytically formulated. The profile of the pressure drop across the length of a fiber can be well represented by a forth-order polynominal fitting. When the pressure buildup ( p * ) is less than 10% of the transmembrane pressure, it is proportional to the squared length of the fiber, and the intrinsic permeance of the membrane, but inversely proportional to the inner diameter to the forth power. The effectiveness factor of the transmembrane pressure is proposed to characterize the effect of the pressure drop. It was found that the effectiveness factor is dominantly determined by a dimensionless parameter ∆ , and it serves as the index measuring the significance of the effect of the pressure drop in hollow fibers. To avoid the unfavorable effect of the pressure drop, the parameter ∆ should be less than 0.4. This criterion is a very important reference for the rational design of hollow fiber permeators.
